findings.description: assemble() loops range(total_chunks - 1), so it silently drops the final chunk and reassembles every multi-chunk upload truncated and corrupt — congratulations on inventing data loss as a feature.

dest_path = os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, session.filename) trusts the client-supplied filename, so '../../etc/cron.d/evil' or an absolute path escapes UPLOAD_DIR for arbitrary file write — the extension allowlist waves '../../x.pdf' right through.

file_size_ok() and the MAX_FILE_MB cap are defined but never called in the upload flow, and receive_chunk does zero size accounting, so a client can write unbounded bytes to disk (DoS) — the limit is purely decorative.
verify_checksum() is never invoked anywhere, so the integrity check it pretends to provide is theater and assembled files are stored unverified.
verify_checksum reads the entire file into memory with f.read() instead of streaming in blocks, so a 200MB+ file is a per-request memory spike inviting OOM under concurrency.

self._sessions and session.received are mutated across start/receive_chunk/assemble/abort with no locking, so concurrent requests race and two callers can both pass is_complete and double-assemble — the code assumes a single-threaded fantasy no upload server actually lives in.

receive_chunk writes the chunk with no error handling, so a mid-write failure (disk full) propagates and leaves an orphan partial chunk on disk that nothing ever cleans up since the session never reaches assemble.
start() with a repeated upload_id silently overwrites the in-progress UploadSession, abandoning its received set and orphaning the already-written chunk files on disk.
the on-disk destination is keyed by client filename, not upload_id, so two distinct uploads sharing a filename clobber each other at the destination path.
os.makedirs in __init__ has exist_ok but no handling for permission/IO errors, so constructing the handler against an unwritable path throws unguarded at startup.
